lens.included_activities: US manufacturers of powered off-road and low-speed specialty transportation equipment, including all-terrain vehicles, snowmobiles, golf carts, similar passenger carriers, and go-carts, with repeat model, parts, accessory, warranty, fleet-support, or service revenue sold to external customers in the lower-middle-market band.
lens.excluded_activities: Animal-drawn vehicle businesses, bespoke race-car builders, one-off fabricators without recurring or repeat customer work, businesses assigned to motorcycles, bicycles, military vehicles, boats, rail, aerospace, or ordinary motor vehicles, and captive units, shells, or financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include dealers, rental and resort fleets, golf courses, commercial sites, recreational users, municipalities, and specialty distributors. Revenue combines vehicles with repeat model orders, replacement parts, accessories, warranty work, fleet support, and sometimes direct maintenance or refurbishment.
lens.deviation_from_default: The residual code combines animal-drawn vehicles, bespoke race cars, and powered recreational or low-speed vehicles, which have incompatible demand and operating models. The lens is narrowed to powered off-road and low-speed specialty vehicles with repeat or aftermarket work for coherence, not attractiveness.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations and wages for NAICS 336900 other transportation equipment manufacturing, a broader group than the narrowed NAICS 336999 lens. I mapped management, business, engineering, computer, design, sales, office, planning, inspection, and diagnostic tasks to partial generative-AI exposure, with little exposure assigned to hands-on assembly, welding, machining, material movement, and repair. The proxy may overstate exposure if the narrowed firms are more assembly-intensive, or understate it if they carry more dealer support, product configuration, and warranty administration.
a.rationale: The broader industry's workforce remains physical: production occupations are 47.50%, installation and repair 9.41%, and transportation and material moving 8.48%. Higher-paid management, business, computer, engineering, sales, office, planning, inspection, and diagnostic work supplies meaningful AI-addressable tasks but is not the majority of labor activity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from payroll and time-study data at representative powered specialty-vehicle manufacturers, separated into production, engineering, dealer support, warranty, sales, and office functions, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The occupation source includes motorcycles, bicycles, and military armored vehicles outside the narrowed lens.
The task mapping estimates exposure rather than observed automation and does not measure firm-level variation in production integration.

rho.rationale: Dealer support, warranty triage, quoting, documentation, procurement, scheduling, and marketing can adopt AI relatively quickly. Engineering, compliance, automated inspection, connected diagnostics, and predictive maintenance require validated product data, sensor coverage, systems integration, cybersecurity, and human sign-off because safety and warranty consequences are material.
rho.change_evidence: A structured survey of eligible firms recording live AI deployments, integration spending, implementation dates, exception rates, warranty outcomes, and verified labor hours would materially move this estimate.
caveats: Manufacturing AI examples establish technical feasibility rather than adoption rates for specialty-vehicle firms.
Safety, emissions, product-liability, dealer-system, and legacy-data constraints are not directly measured.

e.rationale: Powered vehicle makers can generate repeat model, parts, accessories, warranty, fleet, and dealer-support revenue, but the narrowed lens excludes materially different products plus project-only, captive, nontransferable, and out-of-band firms. Distributor-only product sellers may lack a recurring outsourced service relationship even when replacement demand exists.
e.change_evidence: A firm census matching NAICS 336999 companies to product class, normalized EBITDA, parts and service revenue, dealer and fleet relationships, customer concentration, and ownership would determine the eligible share.
caveats: The official industry definition establishes product breadth but not recurring revenue or transferability.
The assigned firm count is a margin-bridged estimate using an auto-parts margin proxy that may not fit the residual industry's product mix.

s5.rationale: Specialty-vehicle manufacturers can attract strategic buyers and face private-company succession pressure, but no product-matched owner-age or completed-control-transfer series was found. Closures without transferable operations and internal restructurings do not qualify.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, or BizBuySell matched to eligible NAICS 336999 firms, combined with owner-age and succession surveys, would materially change the probability.
caveats: This is bounded judgment without observed six-digit control-transfer evidence.
Small private transactions and product-line divestitures are incompletely disclosed.

q.rationale: Brand, product differentiation, dealer networks, parts availability, regulatory knowledge, and fleet uptime can support retention through margin or service quality. Dealer bargaining, model-price competition, rebates, procurement, warranty obligations, and transparent component costs pass a material share of benefits through.
q.change_evidence: Dealer agreements, fleet contracts, warranty terms, discount histories, parts pricing, and gross-margin changes following productivity initiatives would materially change the retained-benefit estimate.
caveats: No source directly measures sharing of AI-enabled benefits.
Retention is likely higher in proprietary parts and diagnostics than in competitively sold new vehicles.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for NAICS 336900 other transportation equipment manufacturing to grow at 2.6% annually through 2034, not constant-quality demand for the narrowed powered NAICS 336999 lens. I converted the broad rate to a five-year ratio but pulled the base lower and widened the interval because the source also includes motorcycles, bicycles, and military armored vehicles. The proxy can overstate narrowed demand if defense or bicycle growth dominates, or understate it if electrified low-speed and fleet vehicles outperform.
d5.rationale: The broad industry projection supports output growth, while the narrowed products serve recreation, fleets, resorts, golf, and specialty mobility. Demand remains discretionary and cyclical for many consumers, and weather, fuel, financing, dealer inventory, and replacement cycles can create large swings. Safety and emissions obligations reinforce product replacement and accountable supply but do not guarantee volume growth.
d5.change_evidence: Product-level unit shipments, dealer inventories, installed fleet counts, replacement ages, order backlogs, and constant-quality forecasts for ATVs, snowmobiles, golf carts, and related low-speed vehicles would materially tighten this ratio.
caveats: The BLS output series is broader than both the six-digit code and narrowed lens.
Real output is not a pure unit measure and may reflect product mix or quality change.
The narrowed products have different seasonality, end markets, and sensitivity to discretionary spending.

o.rationale: Year-five demand remains for physical vehicles, parts, safety accountability, certification, warranty, and fleet uptime. Software can improve configuration, sales, connected diagnostics, and controls but cannot eliminate manufacture and accountable support of the underlying transport equipment.
o.change_evidence: Fleet and dealer interviews plus product forecasts separating physical vehicle and parts demand from software-only services, direct imports, self-service maintenance, and platform-controlled support would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The estimate applies after the demand change in d5 and avoids double counting volume loss.
Direct-to-consumer distribution and embedded remote diagnostics can reduce some dealer or field-service work without replacing physical production.

narrative.executive_view: A coherent screen requires narrowing this residual code to powered off-road and low-speed specialty vehicles with repeat model or aftermarket work. AI can assist engineering, dealer support, warranty, and administration, but production and repair dominate much of the labor base. The addressable firms face real product demand alongside pronounced discretionary and product-cycle risk.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve quoting, product configuration, engineering documentation, procurement, scheduling, marketing, dealer support, warranty triage, visual inspection, and connected diagnostics. Welding, machining, assembly, testing, repair, and material handling remain physical, while safety-critical uses require validated data and human review.
narrative.value_capture: Brands, dealer networks, regulatory know-how, installed fleets, and proprietary parts help retain value. Competitive model pricing, promotions, dealer economics, procurement leverage, warranty commitments, and component-cost transparency limit how much productivity remains with the manufacturer.
narrative.firm_availability: The estimated firm population must be filtered heavily because the code includes incompatible products and the dataset margin bridge uses an auto-parts proxy. A bottom-up map is required to identify businesses with qualifying earnings, recurring or repeat customer work, and transferable dealer or fleet relationships.
narrative.demand_durability: The broad transportation-equipment forecast shows real output growth, but it contains products outside the narrowed lens. ATVs, snowmobiles, golf carts, and low-speed carriers remain physical products with parts and support needs, yet demand can move sharply with weather, leisure spending, financing, dealer inventories, and fleet replacement cycles.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Principal risks include residual-code aggregation, discretionary demand, model obsolescence, electrification investment, battery and component supply, dealer concentration, product recalls, safety liability, emissions compliance, and import competition. Firm eligibility and transfer rates are unobserved, and broad output growth may be driven by excluded segments.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat vehicles, proprietary parts, fleet uptime, and dealer support create durable physical demand plus AI-addressable engineering and service workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: The residual code is highly heterogeneous, and narrowed demand remains cyclical while much of the workforce performs physical production and repair.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 336900 Other Transportation Equipment Manufacturing
sources.supports: Broader-industry occupation mix and wages, including production, repair, engineering, office, business, computer, sales, and material-moving roles.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 336999 All Other Transportation Equipment Manufacturing
sources.supports: Official industry scope and illustrative products, establishing the need to narrow the heterogeneous residual code.

sources.supports: Manufacturing AI applications including automated visual quality control, advanced characterization, operations optimization, and predictive maintenance.

sources.title: Industry Employment and Output Projections, 2024 to 2034
sources.supports: Employment and real-output projection for broader NAICS 336900 used as the demand-growth proxy.

sources.title: All-Terrain Vehicles: Business Guidance
sources.supports: Mandatory ATV safety-standard, certification, labeling, action-plan, and compliance obligations supporting accountable product work.

sources.title: Regulations for Emissions from Recreational Vehicles
